candidates.name: readme-reality-check
candidates.one_liner: A CLI that compares a repo's setup docs against its actual scripts, files, and runnable commands to catch stale onboarding instructions.
deliverables: sample HTML report on real repos
candidates.reasoning: GitHub issues and HN both point to onboarding and documentation drift as a persistent pain point, while agent-heavy repos make setup even harder. A lightweight local auditor is broadly useful, keyless, and very buildable in one session.

candidates.name: agent-run-atlas
candidates.one_liner: A local inspector that turns agent logs, shell transcripts, and file edits into a timeline of what an AI coding agent actually did.
deliverables: demo using synthetic or local run logs
candidates.reasoning: Trending repos are full of agent frameworks, memory systems, and 'subconscious' layers, but the missing piece is transparent local observability. HN interest in agent folder anatomy and workflow visibility suggests strong demand for an explain-what-happened tool.

candidates.name: package-feed-watchdog
candidates.one_liner: A cross-registry scanner that flags suspicious newly published npm, PyPI, and crates packages using metadata and naming heuristics.
deliverables: CLI with table and JSON output
sample report highlighting typosquats and odd descriptions
candidates.reasoning: Recent package feeds are noisy and sometimes openly sketchy, which is a strong signal for a lightweight safety analysis tool. It is self-contained, does not need API keys, and can produce both a useful CLI and an interesting public dataset.

candidates.name: issue-pain-radar
candidates.one_liner: A data product that mines 'good first issue' and 'help wanted' labels to reveal the recurring boring tasks maintainers still lack tools for.
deliverables: top missing-tool opportunities report by ecosystem
candidates.reasoning: Those labels are a goldmine for unmet needs: stale docs, flaky tests, environment setup, repetitive migrations, and tedious content updates. Instead of contributing to the issues, this turns them into a map of what products should exist but mostly do not.

candidates.name: arxiv-weekend-prototypes
candidates.one_liner: A pipeline that converts recent arXiv papers in AI, software engineering, PL, crypto, and data structures into ranked one-session prototype ideas.
deliverables: exportable prompt cards for each idea
candidates.reasoning: There is plenty of research discovery tooling, but much less that filters for 'what can one person actually build tonight.' That gap matters because recent arXiv output is rich, yet most builders need a practical bridge from paper themes to feasible code artifacts.

candidates.name: spec-delta-browser
candidates.one_liner: A CLI and mini web app that turns long-form specs, policies, or laws into semantic diffs, section histories, and blame-like change views.
deliverables: example dataset using a public spec or law repo
candidates.reasoning: HN traction around legislation-as-git shows people love versioned text, but generic tooling for deep spec diffs is still weak. A focused local tool for section-aware change browsing feels fresh enough and can be useful for standards, RFCs, policies, and docs.

candidates.name: terminal-storyboard
candidates.one_liner: A tool that converts raw terminal sessions into compact visual storyboards with command grouping, file impact summaries, and replayable checkpoints.
deliverables: CLI for converting shell transcripts into artifacts
candidates.reasoning: HN interest in git diffs, shell workflows, and agent traces suggests a gap between raw logs and useful narratives. Existing terminal replay tools focus on playback, while a storyboard view would emphasize comprehension, debugging, and sharing how work actually unfolded.

candidates.name: trend-crosswalk
candidates.one_liner: A local trend synthesizer that cross-references GitHub Trending, HN, arXiv, and package feeds to find crowded themes and overlooked buildable gaps.
candidates.reasoning: The strongest meta-pattern across the sources is crowding around agents, memory, and wrappers, while gaps appear in observability, trust, onboarding, and translation from research to practice. A crosswalk tool would itself be useful to indie builders who want signals, not noise.

candidates.name: package-surge-radar
candidates.one_liner: Cross-ecosystem trend analyzer that clusters rising npm, PyPI, and crates.io packages into tool categories and highlights whitespace opportunities.
deliverables: collector for npm, PyPI, and crates.io trend/recent-package pages
taxonomy and scoring pipeline for grouping packages by use case
generated JSON snapshot of rising packages and categories
terminal or static HTML report with 'overcrowded vs underbuilt' insights
candidates.reasoning: Trend pages are noisy and ecosystem-specific; a compact tool that turns package motion into actionable market maps would be useful for builders looking for neglected local-tool categories.

candidates.name: issue-gap-atlas
candidates.one_liner: Data product that mines public 'good first issue' and 'help wanted' tickets to surface repeated missing-tool problems across open source.
deliverables: GitHub issue search collector for selected popular repos
ranked dataset of recurring pain points
report showing which rough edges look like standalone product opportunities
candidates.reasoning: Instead of finding tasks to contribute to, this reframes issue backlogs as demand signals for new products, which is a strong way to discover practical tools with built-in user pain.

candidates.name: sycophancy-smoke-test
candidates.one_liner: CLI and library that scores chat transcripts for over-agreement, missing pushback, and confidence-without-evidence patterns.
deliverables: heuristic scoring engine for affirmation bias and weak disagreement
CLI report with per-turn flags and summary scores
fixture set of good and bad sample conversations
candidates.reasoning: HN and recent research are full of concern about sycophantic AI, but there is room for a lightweight local evaluator that teams can run on transcripts without any model API.

candidates.name: trace-to-tale
candidates.one_liner: CLI that turns strace-style logs into readable narratives of what a program opened, executed, waited on, and failed to find.
deliverables: parser for common strace output patterns
event summarizer that groups syscalls into higher-level actions
ANSI timeline or sequence-style terminal view
candidates.reasoning: There is clear appetite for better low-level tooling and better diffs/explanations; converting raw syscall noise into a human story would make systems debugging much more approachable.

candidates.name: schema-fuzz-studio
candidates.one_liner: Local test generator that produces adversarial JSON payloads from JSON Schema, Pydantic, or Zod-like definitions.
deliverables: schema loader for a practical subset of common schema formats
generator for valid, boundary, and malicious-ish invalid cases
CLI that exports fixtures as JSON files or NDJSON
mini report explaining why each case is interesting
candidates.reasoning: Parser, validation, and type-safety tooling are trending everywhere, and a local-first fuzz generator would be immediately useful to app and library authors without needing cloud services.

candidates.name: graph-evac-lab
candidates.one_liner: Interactive CLI playground for evacuation and routing on time-decaying graphs with sample generators and ASCII visualizations.
deliverables: baseline solver heuristics for evacuation and rerouting
ASCII visualizer for graph state over time
candidates.reasoning: Recent arXiv work in cs.DS suggests interesting dynamic-routing primitives, and packaging them as a small playable simulator would make advanced algorithms tangible and reusable.

candidates.name: paper-primitive-index
candidates.one_liner: Research digestion tool that converts recent arXiv papers into a searchable catalog of implementable primitives, datasets, and toy-project ideas.
deliverables: extractor for titles, abstracts, and inferred primitive types
generated JSON index of papers mapped to buildable concepts
searchable local report for browsing by technique or product angle
candidates.reasoning: Most paper digests stop at summarization; a tool that translates research into concrete hackable building blocks would be valuable for fast prototyping and idea generation.

candidates.name: proof-puzzlebox
candidates.one_liner: Terminal tutor that turns small logic and type-theory proof tasks into puzzle-style exercises with step checking.
deliverables: parser for a tiny propositional logic or lambda-calculus language
interactive terminal solver and hint system
candidates.reasoning: Proof assistants and programming-language topics are resurfacing in mainstream discussion, and a compact puzzle-oriented teaching tool would make those ideas accessible without heavy formal tooling.

selection_note: Top-score tie resolved in favor of the more local-first and lower-risk implementation.
